flashcards.front: How does MapReduce work? Describe the map, shuffle, and reduce phases.
flashcards.back: MapReduce: a programming model for processing large datasets in parallel across a cluster. Input: files on a distributed filesystem (HDFS/GFS). Three phases: (1) Map: for each input record, the user-defined map function emits zero or more key-value pairs. Runs in parallel across all input file blocks. (2) Shuffle (framework-handled): framework collects all values for each key across all mapper outputs, sorts them by key, and routes them to the appropriate reducer. Network-intensive. (3) Reduce: for each unique key, the user-defined reduce function receives the key and all its values, and produces output. Runs in parallel across different keys. Output: written back to distributed filesystem. Key property: input is read-only — failures can be retried safely by re-running the map/reduce on the same input. Fault tolerance: failed tasks are automatically retried on other nodes. Contrast with stream: batch processes bounded input (yesterday's data) on a schedule; stream processes unbounded input continuously.

flashcards.front: Compare reduce-side joins vs. map-side joins in MapReduce. When is each appropriate?
flashcards.back: Reduce-side join (sort-merge join): mapper tags each record with its source dataset; shuffle groups all records with the same key together at the same reducer; reducer performs the join. No assumptions about data size or sort order. Handles any join size. Downside: expensive shuffle across network; all data must be transferred. Map-side join (broadcast hash join): one dataset small enough to fit in memory. The small dataset is loaded as a hash table into every mapper's memory. Each mapper joins its input records against the in-memory hash table locally — no shuffle needed. Much faster for small-large joins. Partitioned map-side join: both datasets are partitioned and sorted the same way. Mapper only needs to hold one partition of the small dataset. Avoids full broadcast. When to use: reduce-side = large-large joins where both datasets are big; broadcast = small-large joins (e.g., user info table < 1 GB, event log is TBs); partitioned map-side = medium-large joins where both are sorted by the same key. Modern systems (Spark, Flink) choose join strategy automatically based on dataset size statistics.

flashcards.front: What limitations of MapReduce did systems like Spark and Flink address?
flashcards.back: MapReduce limitations: (1) Materializes intermediate state: every MapReduce job writes output to HDFS before the next job reads it. For multi-step pipelines (10+ jobs), this creates enormous I/O overhead — slow and expensive. (2) Batch-only: no native streaming support. (3) High latency: even a small query requires a full batch job. Spark improvements: in-memory RDDs (Resilient Distributed Datasets) — intermediate results stay in memory across operations, not written to disk between steps. Lazy evaluation — operations build a logical plan; execution starts only when output is needed, enabling optimization. For fault tolerance: recompute lost partitions from lineage (which operations created them) rather than writing checkpoints. Flink improvements: true streaming-first with bounded (batch) as special case; pipelined execution (operators process data as it arrives, without waiting for upstream to finish); exactly-once semantics with Chandy-Lamport snapshots. Common benefits of both: higher-level APIs (DataFrames, SQL, Dataset); automatic join strategy selection; better resource utilization. MapReduce is now largely deprecated for interactive analytics — Spark/Flink dominate.

puzzles.title: MapReduce Word Count Pipeline
puzzles.instructions: Arrange the steps of a MapReduce word count job.

steps.text: Input file split into blocks across HDFS nodes
Map phase: each mapper reads its block, emits (word, 1) for each word
Combiner (optional local reduce): sum counts per word within each mapper output
Shuffle: framework sorts all (word, count) pairs by word, routes to reducers
Reduce phase: reducer sums all counts for each word, emits (word, total_count)
Output written to HDFS as final word count file

puzzles.hint: The combiner is a local optimization that reduces shuffle data volume but doesn't change correctness.
puzzles.explanation: MapReduce: split → map → (optional combine) → shuffle+sort → reduce → output. The shuffle is the most expensive phase — it moves data across the network.
